title: Domain Model
summary: The aggregates, fields, and invariants behind KH Wholesale — anchored on the FulfillmentGroup (per-supplier sub-order) as the spine that ties ordering, fulfillment, and settlement together.

blocks.text: I modeled KH Wholesale as a set of strict bounded contexts sharing one Postgres database, and the single decision that shaped everything downstream was splitting every buyer Order into one FulfillmentGroup per supplier.

paragraphs: KH is a managed marketplace: a buyer fills one Cart that can span many suppliers, and KH orchestrates pricing, credit, inventory, fulfillment, and settlement behind it. That single-cart-many-suppliers reality is the central modeling tension. A buyer thinks in terms of one Order; a supplier only ever acts on the part of that Order that belongs to them; and settlement has to pay each supplier independently for exactly the lines they fulfilled. If you model only the buyer-level Order, every one of those concerns leaks into ad-hoc filtering and conditional logic.

So the spine of the domain is the FulfillmentGroup — the per-supplier sub-order created when an Order is placed. The Order is a thin buyer-level header whose status is derived, not stored authoritatively; the FulfillmentGroup is where real work happens. Suppliers accept or reject a FulfillmentGroup, inventory is reserved per FulfillmentGroup line, Shipments are created against a FulfillmentGroup, and a Settlement is computed per FulfillmentGroup. An OrderLine therefore belongs to exactly one FulfillmentGroup (never directly to the Order), which makes ownership unambiguous at every layer: RBAC scoping, the supplier portal's work queue, the carrier handoff, and the payout ledger all key off the same aggregate.

The ubiquitous language is deliberately small and shared across all three frontends (buyer storefront, supplier portal, ops admin) and the backend modules. An Organization is either a buyer or a supplier (distinguished by org_type); Users belong to orgs through a Membership that carries a Role; a Sku is the sellable unit and always belongs to one supplier Organization, which is what makes the per-supplier split mechanical rather than heuristic — we group a Cart's items by sku.supplier_org_id. Money is integer KRW stored as bigint, and prices are snapshotted onto the OrderLine at placement so the amount a buyer agreed to is immutable regardless of later price-list changes. State lives in explicit, table-driven state machines rather than scattered booleans.

entities.name: Organization
entities.description: A buyer OR a supplier tenant. The root of row-level isolation — almost every other row carries org_id and is scoped to it.

fields.name: business_registration_no
fields.note: validated via NTS gov API during onboarding

fields.name: credit_limit_krw
fields.note: buyers only; checked synchronously at checkout

fields.name: status
fields.note: 'active' | 'suspended'; suppliers reach 'active' only via the approval workflow

relations: 1 Organization has many Memberships (its Users)
A supplier Organization owns many Skus
A buyer Organization owns many Carts and Orders

entities.name: Membership
entities.description: The User-to-Organization join that carries the Role. RBAC and tenant scoping resolve through Membership.
relations: Many Memberships per User (a user may belong to several orgs)
UNIQUE (user_id, org_id) — one membership per user per org

entities.name: Sku
entities.description: The sellable unit. Belongs to exactly one supplier Organization — this ownership is what drives the per-supplier Order split.

fields.name: version
fields.note: monotonic; used as the per-SKU version guard in the OpenSearch projection

relations: Many Skus per Product
1 Sku has many InventoryItems (one per location/DC)
Referenced by CartItem, OrderLine, StockReservation

entities.name: ContractPrice
entities.description: Buyer-specific negotiated price for a Sku, overriding the supplier's PriceList. Resolved during pricing snapshot at placement and re-checked at supplier confirm.
relations: Falls back to PriceList when no active ContractPrice exists for (buyer, sku)
Resolved value is snapshotted onto OrderLine.unit_price_krw

entities.name: Order
entities.description: Buyer-level header. Intentionally thin: its status is DERIVED from the statuses of its FulfillmentGroups, not stored as the source of truth.

fields.name: status
fields.note: DRAFT -> PLACED -> derived PARTIALLY_* / COMPLETED / CANCELLED

fields.name: total_krw
fields.note: sum of snapshotted OrderLine extended amounts

relations: 1 Order is SPLIT into many FulfillmentGroups (one per distinct supplier in the cart)
Order has no direct OrderLines — lines hang off FulfillmentGroups

entities.name: FulfillmentGroup
entities.description: THE SPINE. The per-supplier sub-order that suppliers actually act on; the unit of inventory reservation, shipment, and settlement.

fields.name: status
fields.note: AWAITING_SUPPLIER -> ACCEPTED -> FULFILLING -> SHIPPED -> DELIVERED -> SETTLED; branches REJECTED, CANCELLED, PARTIALLY_SHIPPED

fields.name: accepted_at
fields.note: null until supplier accepts; re-price/validate happens here

relations: Many FulfillmentGroups per Order; UNIQUE (order_id, supplier_org_id)
1 FulfillmentGroup has many OrderLines
1 FulfillmentGroup has many Shipments and at most one Settlement

entities.name: OrderLine
entities.description: A single line of demand. Belongs to exactly one FulfillmentGroup. Carries the immutable price snapshot.

fields.name: fulfillment_group_id
fields.note: FK -> FulfillmentGroup (NOT NULL; never points at Order directly)

relations: Exactly one FulfillmentGroup per OrderLine
References one Sku; mirrors to ShipmentLine when shipped

entities.name: InventoryItem
entities.description: Authoritative stock per Sku per location/DC. The database — not the app — is the source of truth for availability.

fields.name: available
fields.note: CHECK (available >= 0) — guarantees no oversell at the DB

fields.name: version
fields.note: optimistic-lock version; bumped under SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on confirm

relations: UNIQUE (sku_id, location_id)
Decremented atomically when a StockReservation is confirmed at allocation

entities.name: StockReservation
entities.description: Two-phase hold. Phase 1 is a Redis soft-hold with TTL at checkout; phase 2 is this confirmed Postgres row, written under a row lock at supplier accept/allocation.

fields.name: expires_at
fields.note: swept by a reservation-expiry job; partial UNIQUE on active reservations

relations: Confirms against an InventoryItem under SELECT ... FOR UPDATE
Locks acquired in sku_id order to prevent deadlocks on multi-line orders

entities.name: Shipment
entities.description: A physical dispatch against a FulfillmentGroup. One group may split into several Shipments by DC and by partial availability.

fields.name: carrier_id
fields.note: FK -> Carrier; handed off via a per-carrier adapter

fields.name: status
fields.note: CREATED -> ALLOCATED -> PICKING -> PACKED -> DISPATCHED -> IN_TRANSIT -> DELIVERED; branches EXCEPTION, RETURNED

fields.name: tracking_no
fields.note: carrier reference; TrackingEvents ingested via webhook

fields.name: proof_of_delivery_s3_key
fields.note: photo/signature stored in S3 on DELIVERED

relations: Many Shipments per FulfillmentGroup
1 Shipment has many ShipmentLines and TrackingEvents, at most one ProofOfDelivery

entities.name: SupplierApplication
entities.description: Drives the maker-checker onboarding workflow. Every state change is recorded append-only as an ApprovalDecision.

fields.name: status
fields.note: APPLIED -> DOCS_SUBMITTED -> AUTOMATED_CHECKS -> RISK_REVIEW -> COMPLIANCE_APPROVAL -> CATALOG_ONBOARDING -> ACTIVATED; branches INFO_REQUESTED, REJECTED

relations: 1 SupplierApplication has many ApprovalTasks and append-only ApprovalDecisions
On ACTIVATED, sets up the supplier's PriceList and initial Skus

entities.name: Settlement
entities.description: Computed once per delivered FulfillmentGroup. Fees and rounding applied here; posts double-entry LedgerEntries and a Payout.

fields.name: fulfillment_group_id
fields.note: FK -> FulfillmentGroup; UNIQUE (one settlement per group)

fields.name: fee_krw
fields.note: rounding applied ONLY here, per the single documented rule

fields.name: net_payout_krw
fields.note: gross_krw - fee_krw; settled on KST business-day cutoffs

relations: Exactly one Settlement per FulfillmentGroup (UNIQUE)
1 Settlement produces many LedgerEntries and one Payout

entities.name: LedgerEntry
entities.description: Append-only, double-entry accounting line. Never updated or deleted — corrections are new reversing entries.
relations: UNIQUE (fulfillment_group_id, entry_type) — guards against double settlement
Many LedgerEntries per Settlement

entities.name: OutboxEvent
entities.description: Written in the same DB transaction as the state change it describes, then relayed to SQS/SNS. The backbone of all async work; idempotent consumers dedup on it.

fields.name: aggregate_id
fields.note: e.g. the FulfillmentGroup or Order id

fields.name: published_at
fields.note: null until the relay publishes to SQS

relations: Written atomically with the aggregate change (no dual writes)
Consumed by search-projection, settlement, and notifications workers

blocks.title: Why FulfillmentGroup is the spine
blocks.body: The buyer-level Order is a derived view; the FulfillmentGroup is the real aggregate. Suppliers accept/reject it, inventory reserves per its lines, Shipments and Settlements key off it, and RBAC scopes to it. Because a Sku always belongs to exactly one supplier Organization, the Cart-to-FulfillmentGroup split is a mechanical group-by on sku.supplier_org_id rather than a heuristic. Modeling this split on day one — instead of bolting per-supplier logic onto a flat Order later — is the decision I would make first again.

items: An OrderLine belongs to exactly one FulfillmentGroup and never references the Order directly; the Order is just the buyer-level header.
An Order is SPLIT into exactly one FulfillmentGroup per distinct supplier (UNIQUE (order_id, supplier_org_id)); a Sku belongs to exactly one supplier, so the split is deterministic.
Order.status is DERIVED from its FulfillmentGroups' statuses — it is never the authoritative source of truth.
OrderLine.unit_price_krw is snapshotted at placement from the resolved ContractPrice/PriceList and is immutable; search is never authoritative for money — checkout always re-prices from Postgres.
All money is integer KRW stored as bigint; rounding is applied only at fee calculation in Settlement, per a single documented rule.
InventoryItem.available can never go negative (CHECK (available >= 0)); availability is authoritative at the database, reserved via Redis soft-hold then confirmed under SELECT ... FOR UPDATE.
Reservation locks are acquired in sku_id order to give consistent lock ordering and avoid deadlocks on multi-line orders.
Exactly one Settlement per FulfillmentGroup, and LedgerEntry is UNIQUE per (fulfillment_group_id, entry_type) — these constraints make double settlement impossible.
LedgerEntry and ApprovalDecision are append-only: no UPDATE or DELETE; corrections are new reversing/decision rows.
OutboxEvent is written in the same transaction as the change it describes (no dual writes); consumers are idempotent and dedup on its key.
Maker-checker is structurally enforced: a SupplierApplication's compliance_reviewer_id (checker) must differ from its risk_reviewer_id (maker).
Every tenant-scoped row carries org_id and is filtered by row-level scoping, so a buyer or supplier can only ever see its own data.
